attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

The user in the primary session is asking about a UI layout issue in `ReconcileCascade`. The problem is that when removing one of two wanted posters, because `cc.Layout` is used for positioning, there's a sudden position shift. Someone mentioned this looks abrupt, and the user is asking for ideas on solutions to make the display look better, explicitly requesting NOT to modify anything directly.

Looking at the tool executions:
1. Several Glob operations to find WantedPoster-related files
2. Read operations on:
   - WantedPosterManager.ts - This shows the ReconcileCascade method around line 419
   - WantedPosterItem.ts - Shows the item component
   - WantedPosterItem.prefab - Shows the prefab structure
3. Grep operations to find layout-related properties and method usage

From the code I can see:
- `ReconcileCascade` handles cascade updates for wanted posters
- It uses `cc.Layout` component for positioning items
- When items are removed, the layout automatically repositions remaining items
- The sudden shift is because Layout immediately recalculates positions when child nodes become inactive

This is a discovery phase - the primary Claude is investigating the codebase to understand the problem before proposing solutions. The user explicitly said "請勿直接修改" (please do not modify directly), so this is purely exploratory work.

Since this is just investigation/discovery work with no actual changes made, I should record this as a discovery observation about the layout behavior issue being investigated.

  <narrative>Investigation revealed that the WantedPoster system uses Cocos Creator's cc.Layout component to manage positioning of wanted poster items. The ReconcileCascade method handles updates during cascade events by activating/deactivating poster items and playing In/Out animations. When one poster is removed from a set of two, the Layout component immediately recalculates positions for remaining active children, causing a visible position shift. The system has separate layout configurations for portrait and landscape orientations (WantedPosterLayoutConfig), with properties like paddingLeft/Right/Top/Bottom, spacingX/Y, and alignment settings. The abruptness occurs because Layout repositioning happens synchronously while the Out animation plays, creating a visual discontinuity. Alternative solutions under consideration include: deferring layout updates until after animations complete, using manual position tweening instead of Layout, implementing ghost/placeholder nodes to maintain spacing, or adding smooth position transitions when layout changes occur.</narrative>

  <title>WantedPoster animation flow and state management architecture</title>
  <subtitle>WantedPosterState orchestrates bounty animations via events while Manager handles UI layout and item lifecycle</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>WantedPosterState manages gameplay logic for triggered bounties during elimination cascades</fact>
    <fact>Animation sequence is Mark → Symbol_In → (Mark_Active ∥ Symbol_Active) → multiplier base update</fact>
    <fact>State dispatches PLAY_MARK and PLAY_MARK_ACTIVE events to WantedPosterManager which handles persistent poster items</fact>
    <fact>Bounties with identical BaseSymbol are grouped together and their Multiple values are multiplied for display</fact>
    <fact>EffectPlate.SpawnAndPlayWantedSymbolIn creates temporary spine nodes at world positions for on-field display</fact>
    <fact>Layout repositioning happens in Manager's ReconcileCascade when posters are added/removed during state transitions</fact>
    <fact>Wild positions are filtered out before spawning wanted poster spines to avoid duplicate visual effects</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The WantedPoster system uses an event-driven architecture separating concerns between state management (WantedPosterState) and UI management (WantedPosterManager). When bounties are triggered during cascade elimination, WantedPosterState groups them by BaseSymbol, computes combined multipliers, and orchestrates a multi-phase animation sequence. It communicates with WantedPosterManager via event dispatches (PLAY_MARK, PLAY_MARK_ACTIVE) which target persistent poster items in the UI. Simultaneously, it spawns temporary spine nodes on the game field at world positions where symbols were eliminated. The layout repositioning issue occurs in WantedPosterManager.ReconcileCascade, which is called during state transitions when the bounty list changes. The Manager activates/deactivates poster items and cc.Layout immediately recalculates positions for remaining active children, causing the abrupt shift. The State doesn't directly control layout timing - it only triggers data updates via SET_WANTED_POSTER_DATA events. This separation means any layout smoothing solution must be implemented in the Manager layer, not the State layer.</narrative>

  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Cascade reconciliation flow determines poster persistence via visible symbols</title>
  <subtitle>MatchingPatchUpState computes visibleBaseSymbols from non-eliminated positions to guide ReconcileCascade persistence logic</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>MatchingPatchUpState.OnEnter dispatches SET_WANTED_POSTER_DATA after each cascade plate transition</fact>
    <fact>ComputeVisibleBaseSymbols method excludes EliminatePos positions and collects remaining symbol IDs on the plate</fact>
    <fact>visibleBaseSymbols parameter is passed to ReconcileCascade to determine if same BaseSymbol posters should persist</fact>
    <fact>ReconcileCascade BC-25 logic: if BaseSymbol exists in visibleBaseSymbols and Multiple matches, poster persists without animation</fact>
    <fact>ReconcileCascade BC-24 logic: if BaseSymbol not in visibleBaseSymbols, poster exits then re-enters even if Multiple unchanged</fact>
    <fact>Node_WantedPoster.prefab contains ItemRoot with cc.Layout managing two WantedPosterItem instances</fact>
    <fact>Layout is configured with _layoutType=1 (VERTICAL), spacingX=-10, and _isAlign=true</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>During cascade elimination sequences, MatchingPatchUpState coordinates the wanted poster updates by computing which BaseSymbols remain visible after eliminations. The ComputeVisibleBaseSymbols method iterates through PlateSymbol grid positions, excluding any position listed in EliminatePos, and collects the unique symbol IDs that remain on screen. This set is passed as the third parameter to SET_WANTED_POSTER_DATA event with isNewRound=false to signal a cascade update. WantedPosterManager.ReconcileCascade uses this information to make persistence decisions: if a bounty's BaseSymbol appears in visibleBaseSymbols AND the Multiple value matches, the poster item stays active and skips animations (BC-25 continuous display). If the BaseSymbol is absent from visibleBaseSymbols, the poster exits and re-enters even with matching Multiple (BC-24). When posters are deactivated, cc.Layout's updateLayout runs synchronously, causing immediate repositioning of remaining active children. The layout shift timing conflict occurs because poster Out animations may still be playing while Layout has already repositioned siblings to fill the gap.</narrative>
